past_medical_history: Episode of bilateral lower extremity numbness and tingling lasting approximately 4 weeks, 8 months ago, evaluated in urgent care and attributed to lumbar radiculopathy; no imaging was obtained
Intermittent fatigue over the past year, attributed to work-related stress
family_history: Mother with type 2 diabetes and hypothyroidism
Father with hypertension, age 64, otherwise healthy
Maternal grandmother with systemic lupus erythematosus
No family history of multiple sclerosis or other demyelinating diseases

social_history.alcohol: Rare social drinker, 1-2 drinks per month
social_history.living_situation: Lives with husband and two children (ages 5 and 8) in a single-family home
social_history.exercise: Walks 2-3 miles several times per week, occasional cycling

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert, oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Speech is fluent without dysarthria or aphasia. Language comprehension, naming, and repetition intact. Attention and concentration intact to serial sevens. Affect is calm but concerned about symptoms.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: CN II: Visual acuity reduced in the right eye to 20/70 (left eye 20/20). Right relative afferent pupillary defect (Marcus Gunn pupil) present. Color desaturation noted in the right eye using Ishihara plates (7/14 correct right eye, 14/14 left eye). Fundoscopy reveals mild pallor of the right optic disc. CN III-VI: Extraocular movements full bilaterally. No nystagmus. No ptosis. CN V: Sensation intact bilaterally. CN VII: No facial asymmetry. CN VIII-XII: Intact.
neurological_exam.motor: Upper extremities: 5/5 bilaterally in deltoids, biceps, triceps, wrist extensors and flexors, grip strength, and interossei. Lower extremities: 4+/5 bilateral hip flexion, 4+/5 right knee extension, otherwise 5/5. No pronator drift. No fasciculations or muscle atrophy noted.
neurological_exam.sensory: Decreased vibration sense in both lower extremities to the level of the tibial tuberosities. Decreased light touch and pinprick sensation over bilateral lower extremities in a non-dermatomal pattern. Reduced joint position sense in the right great toe. Temperature sensation grossly intact. Upper extremity sensation intact.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally. Triceps 2+ bilaterally. Brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally. Patellar 3+ bilaterally (brisk). Achilles 3+ bilaterally. Bilateral extensor plantar responses (positive Babinski sign bilaterally). Sustained ankle clonus (3-4 beats) bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing: mild dysmetria on the right with terminal intention tremor. Heel-to-shin: mildly impaired bilaterally with slight lateral deviation. Rapid alternating movements mildly slowed on the right.
neurological_exam.gait: Mildly spastic gait with slight circumduction of the right lower extremity. Tandem gait impaired with tendency to veer rightward. Romberg sign positive. Unable to perform tandem walk without assistance.
neurological_exam.additional: Lhermitte sign positive (electric shock-like sensation radiating down the spine and into both legs with neck flexion). Uhthoff phenomenon reported by patient (worsening of visual symptoms with hot showers). No meningismus. Estimated EDSS 3.0.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive blurring of vision in the right eye with pain behind the eye for 4 days
patient.history_present_illness: Ms. Denise Carter is a 37-year-old right-handed African American woman who presents to the emergency department with a 4-day history of progressive visual loss in her right eye accompanied by a deep, aching retro-orbital pain that worsens with eye movements, particularly lateral gaze and upward gaze. She initially noticed mild blurring of her right visual field while grading papers four evenings ago, which she attributed to eye strain after prolonged screen use. Over the subsequent 48 hours, the blurring progressed substantially, and she began to notice that colors, especially reds and greens, appeared faded or washed out when she covered her left eye and looked solely through the right. The retro-orbital pain developed on the second day and has been constant, rated at 5 out of 10 in intensity, worsened by eye movement but not by bright light. By the morning of presentation, she described her right eye vision as looking through a frosted window, with only vague outlines visible, which prompted her husband to bring her to the emergency department. On further questioning, she reports that approximately eight months ago she experienced a prolonged episode of numbness and tingling affecting both lower extremities from the mid-thigh downward, which developed over two to three days and persisted for roughly four weeks before gradually resolving. She visited an urgent care clinic at that time and was told it was likely a pinched nerve in her back, but no imaging studies were obtained. She notes that she still occasionally experiences brief tingling in her feet, particularly in the evenings or when she is fatigued. She also describes an unusual phenomenon in which her vision becomes noticeably worse when she takes hot showers or after vigorous exercise, though it returns to baseline after cooling down. Over the past several months, she reports progressive fatigue that is disproportionate to her activity level, difficulty concentrating during afternoon classes, and an episode approximately three months ago where her right leg felt unusually heavy and stiff while walking up stairs, which resolved after resting for several minutes. She denies any fevers, unintentional weight loss, joint pain, skin rashes, oral ulcers, dry eyes, or dry mouth. She denies any history of seizures, difficulty swallowing, or bowel or bladder incontinence, although she notes increased urinary frequency over the past two months that she had attributed to drinking more coffee.

findings.size: Multiple lesions ranging from 4 mm to 16 mm

signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Hyperintense with ovoid morphology oriented perpendicular to the ventricular surface (Dawson fingers configuration)

findings.location: Juxtacortical white matter, bilateral frontal lobes and left parietal lobe
Right middle cerebellar peduncle and left brachium pontis (infratentorial)
Right optic nerve, intraorbital and intracanalicular segments

signal_characteristics.T2_fat_sat: Hyperintense signal within right optic nerve with mild swelling

findings.mass_effect: Mild swelling of the right optic nerve
findings.borders: Diffuse involvement of the affected segment

findings.location: Left periventricular white matter, adjacent to frontal horn of lateral ventricle
findings.size: 12 mm with open ring enhancement pattern

signal_characteristics.T1_post_contrast: Incomplete ring enhancement, open toward cortical surface

findings.location: Cervical spinal cord at C2-C3 level, dorsolateral columns predominantly right-sided
findings.size: Less than one vertebral segment in craniocaudal extent

signal_characteristics.T2: Hyperintense, involving less than half the cross-sectional area of the cord

additional_observations: Normal ventricular size and morphology without hydrocephalus
No longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis (spinal cord lesion spans less than 1 vertebral segment)
No involvement of the area postrema, periaqueductal gray, or hypothalamic regions
mri.impression: Multiple demyelinating lesions distributed across periventricular, juxtacortical, infratentorial, and spinal cord regions. The findings satisfy the 2017 McDonald criteria for dissemination in space (all four characteristic CNS regions involved) and dissemination in time (simultaneous enhancing and non-enhancing lesions). Clinical correlation recommended.

special_tests.Oligoclonal bands: Positive — 5 bands unique to CSF, not present in matched serum sample

csf.interpretation: Opening pressure: 15 cmH2O. WBC: 12 cells/microL (reference: 0-5) (N/A PMN/N/A lymph). Protein: 48 mg/dL (reference: 15-45 mg/dL). Glucose: 58 mg/dL (reference: 40-70 mg/dL) (ratio 0.66 (serum glucose 88 mg/dL)). Oligoclonal bands: Positive — 5 bands unique to CSF, not present in matched serum sample. IgG index: 0.78 (elevated; reference: <0.70). IgG synthesis rate: 10.8 mg/day (elevated; reference: <8.0 mg/day). Myelin basic protein: 5.2 ng/mL (elevated; reference: <4.0 ng/mL). Cytology: No malignant cells identified. VDRL: Non-reactive. HSV PCR: Negative. West Nile virus IgM: Negative. West Nile virus IgG: Negative.

output.impression: Abnormal values: Right eye P100 latency 134 ms ms (H), Right eye P100 amplitude 2.8 microV microV (H), Interocular P100 latency difference 37 ms ms (H).

findings.location: Cervical spinal cord at C2-C3, dorsolateral columns, right-sided
findings.size: Less than one vertebral segment in craniocaudal extent

signal_characteristics.T2: Hyperintense, involving less than half the cross-sectional area of the cord

findings.location: Thoracic spinal cord at T4-T5, posterior columns bilateral
findings.size: Less than one vertebral segment in craniocaudal extent

signal_characteristics.T1_post_contrast: Faint peripheral enhancement suggesting subacute activity

findings.location: Thoracic spinal cord at T9, lateral column, left-sided

additional_observations: No longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis (all lesions span less than one vertebral segment)
No compressive myelopathy or disc herniation
Normal conus medullaris and cauda equina
output.impression: Three short-segment spinal cord lesions identified: C2-C3 (non-enhancing, chronic), T4-T5 (faintly enhancing, subacute), and T9 (non-enhancing, chronic). The absence of longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis argues strongly against NMOSD. Clinical correlation recommended.

Abnormal values: Peripapillary RNFL thickness (average) 68 micrometers micrometers (H), RNFL temporal quadrant 44 micrometers micrometers (H), Ganglion cell-inner plexiform layer (GCIPL) 64 micrometers micrometers (H).

findings.location: Left periventricular white matter, adjacent to the posterior horn of the lateral ventricle

volumetrics.resolving_lesions: 2 previously enhancing lesions no longer enhance

additional_observations: No new spinal cord lesions identified on available sagittal cervical sequences
Previously noted lesions are stable in size
No evidence of progressive brain atrophy
output.impression: Follow-up MRI at 3 months demonstrates one new small enhancing periventricular lesion, indicating ongoing subclinical disease activity. Prior enhancing lesions in the right optic nerve and left periventricular white matter have resolved. Clinical correlation recommended.

results.title: Diagnosis of multiple sclerosis: 2017 revisions of the McDonald criteria
results.authors: Thompson AJ, Banwell BL, Barkhof F, et al.
results.key_findings: Revised criteria allow CSF-specific oligoclonal bands to substitute for dissemination in time in patients with a single clinical attack and DIS. Symptomatic and asymptomatic MRI lesions can both count. Cortical lesions are now accepted alongside juxtacortical lesions for DIS.

results.title: MRI criteria for the diagnosis of multiple sclerosis: MAGNIMS consensus guidelines
results.authors: Filippi M, Rocca MA, Ciccarelli O, et al.
results.key_findings: Dissemination in space requires at least 1 T2 lesion in at least 2 of 4 CNS areas: periventricular, cortical or juxtacortical, infratentorial, and spinal cord.

output.summary: The 2017 McDonald criteria require demonstration of dissemination in space (DIS) and dissemination in time (DIT). DIS is met by T2 lesions in at least 2 of 4 characteristic CNS locations. DIT can be shown by simultaneous enhancing and non-enhancing lesions on a single MRI, or by new lesions on follow-up MRI, or by CSF-specific oligoclonal bands. This patient meets DIS with lesions in all 4 characteristic locations and DIT with simultaneous enhancing and non-enhancing lesions, as well as CSF oligoclonal bands.

output.proposed: Ocrelizumab 300 mg IV initial dose, then 600 mg IV every 6 months
contraindications: History of life-threatening infusion reaction to ocrelizumab
warnings: Screen for hepatitis B and C before initiation
Premedicate with methylprednisolone 100 mg IV, diphenhydramine, and acetaminophen before each infusion
Risk of infusion-related reactions, typically with first infusion
Increased risk of infections — monitor for signs and symptoms
Risk of progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML), though lower than with natalizumab
Live vaccines should be administered at least 6 weeks before initiation
Not recommended during pregnancy — effective contraception recommended
output.formulary_status: Formulary, prior authorization required, specialty pharmacy
alternatives: Natalizumab (high efficacy, requires JCV antibody monitoring, monthly infusion)
Dimethyl fumarate (moderate efficacy, oral, GI side effects and lymphopenia risk)
Fingolimod (moderate-high efficacy, oral, first-dose cardiac monitoring required)
Glatiramer acetate (lower efficacy, injection, favorable safety profile)

output.query: NMOSD versus multiple sclerosis differentiation diagnostic criteria

results.title: International consensus diagnostic criteria for neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorders
results.authors: Wingerchuk DM, Banwell B, Bennett JL, et al.
results.key_findings: NMOSD with AQP4-IgG positive requires at least one core clinical characteristic (optic neuritis, acute myelitis, area postrema syndrome, brainstem syndrome, diencephalic syndrome, or cerebral syndrome). AQP4-IgG negative NMOSD requires more stringent clinical and MRI criteria. Longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis (>=3 vertebral segments) and area postrema involvement are hallmarks.

results.title: Distinguishing multiple sclerosis from AQP4-NMOSD and MOG-antibody disease
results.key_findings: Key distinguishing features of MS vs NMOSD include: MS has periventricular Dawson fingers and short spinal cord lesions, while NMOSD has LETM and area postrema lesions. Oligoclonal bands present in >90% of MS but only ~20% of NMOSD. AQP4-IgG seropositivity is highly specific for NMOSD.

output.summary: The differentiation between MS and NMOSD hinges on several key features. MS typically presents with periventricular ovoid lesions (Dawson fingers), short-segment spinal cord lesions (<3 vertebral segments), and CSF oligoclonal bands (>90% of cases). NMOSD is characterized by longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis (>=3 segments), area postrema involvement, and AQP4-IgG positivity. In this patient, short-segment spinal lesions, Dawson fingers morphology, positive oligoclonal bands, and negative AQP4-IgG strongly favor MS over NMOSD.

differential.key_features: AQP4 antibodies negative, short-segment spinal cord lesions (not longitudinally extensive), periventricular Dawson fingers morphology characteristic of MS, no area postrema or periaqueductal gray involvement
MOG antibodies negative, optic disc pallor rather than prominent disc edema, CSF oligoclonal bands present (less common in MOGAD), periventricular lesion pattern typical of MS
GFAP antibodies negative, no periventricular radial linear enhancement pattern, no leptomeningeal enhancement, lesion distribution classic for MS
Normal ESR and CRP, negative ANA, Dawson fingers morphology not seen in vasculitis, lesion distribution and open ring enhancement pattern typical of demyelination
No leptomeningeal enhancement, normal ACE level, no pulmonary or systemic findings, lesion pattern typical of MS not sarcoidosis

optimal_actions.action: Obtain detailed history including prior neurological symptoms suggesting dissemination in time and heat sensitivity (Uhthoff phenomenon)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: History of bilateral lower extremity numbness 8 months ago (prior relapse) and visual worsening with heat exposure

optimal_actions.action: Perform comprehensive neurological examination with attention to optic nerve function, upper motor neuron signs, sensory levels, and coordination
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Right RAPD, reduced right visual acuity, bilateral Babinski signs, brisk reflexes, positive Lhermitte sign, sensory deficits in lower extremities, cerebellar signs

optimal_actions.action: Order MRI brain and cervical spine with gadolinium contrast
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Multiple demyelinating lesions in periventricular, juxtacortical, infratentorial, and spinal cord regions with enhancing and non-enhancing lesions meeting 2017 McDonald criteria for DIS and DIT

optimal_actions.action: Order basic labs including CBC, BMP, ESR, CRP, ANA, vitamin B12, TSH, NMO-IgG, MOG antibodies, and ACE to exclude mimics
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal labs except low vitamin D; negative AQP4, MOG antibodies, and normal ACE

optimal_actions.action: Perform lumbar puncture with CSF analysis including oligoclonal bands, IgG index, cell count, protein, glucose, and infectious studies
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Oligoclonal bands present, elevated IgG index, mild lymphocytic pleocytosis, elevated myelin basic protein, negative infectious studies

optimal_actions.action: Initiate IV methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 3-5 days for acute optic neuritis
Communicate diagnosis of relapsing-remitting MS to patient and discuss disease-modifying therapy options
Initiate high-efficacy disease-modifying therapy (e.g., ocrelizumab or natalizumab) given evidence of active disease with multiple relapses
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Selected DMT is appropriate for patient profile, disease activity level, and reproductive considerations

optimal_actions.action: Order visual evoked potentials to document baseline optic nerve conduction
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Prolonged P100 latency in right eye confirming demyelinating optic neuropathy

optimal_actions.action: Order MRI thoracic spine with contrast to complete spinal cord survey
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Additional short-segment spinal cord lesions at T4-T5 and T9

optimal_actions.action: Supplement vitamin D (2000-5000 IU daily) given deficiency
Schedule follow-up MRI in 3 months to establish new baseline on disease-modifying therapy

critical_actions: Obtain brain MRI with gadolinium to evaluate for demyelinating lesions and assess McDonald criteria for DIS and DIT
Perform lumbar puncture to assess for oligoclonal bands and elevated IgG index
Check NMO-IgG (aquaporin-4 antibodies) and MOG antibodies to exclude NMOSD and MOGAD
Treat acute optic neuritis with IV methylprednisolone
Initiate disease-modifying therapy after confirming MS diagnosis
contraindicated_actions: Do not start chronic oral corticosteroids as maintenance therapy for MS (ineffective and carries significant long-term side effects)
Do not perform brain biopsy — diagnosis is readily achievable through non-invasive criteria (McDonald 2017)
Do not delay disease-modifying therapy while waiting for spontaneous remission of optic neuritis
Do not initiate natalizumab without first checking JC virus antibody status
Do not administer high-dose IV thiamine empirically without clinical evidence of Wernicke encephalopathy — this patient lacks risk factors for thiamine deficiency
key_reasoning_points: The combination of acute right optic neuritis with a prior episode of bilateral lower extremity numbness 8 months ago establishes clinical dissemination in time with at least two distinct relapsing episodes
MRI demonstrates dissemination in space across all four characteristic MS locations: periventricular (Dawson fingers), juxtacortical, infratentorial (cerebellar peduncle), and spinal cord
Simultaneous presence of enhancing and non-enhancing lesions on the initial MRI provides radiographic dissemination in time, satisfying 2017 McDonald criteria independently of clinical history
CSF oligoclonal bands (5 bands unique to CSF) and elevated IgG index provide additional confirmatory evidence and can substitute for DIT under 2017 McDonald criteria
Negative AQP4 and MOG antibodies are essential to exclude NMOSD and MOGAD, which require different treatment strategies
The short-segment spinal cord lesion (less than one vertebral segment) favors MS over NMOSD, which typically produces longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis
Uhthoff phenomenon (worsening of visual symptoms with heat) is a classic feature of MS caused by temperature-dependent conduction failure in demyelinated axons
Lhermitte sign (electric sensation down the spine with neck flexion) indicates cervical cord demyelination and is highly suggestive of MS in this clinical context
Open ring enhancement pattern on MRI is highly specific for demyelination and helps distinguish from tumefactive lesions or other etiologies

metadata.difficulty_description: Straightforward case with classic optic neuritis presentation, prior relapsing episode, and diagnostic MRI/CSF findings meeting McDonald 2017 criteria. Minimal confounders.
metadata.mcdonald_criteria_met: 2017 McDonald criteria for DIS (all 4 regions) and DIT (simultaneous enhancing and non-enhancing lesions, plus CSF OCBs) both satisfied
metadata.educational_value: Classic RRMS presentation with optic neuritis and prior sensory relapse, requiring differentiation from NMOSD given the source case context involving NMOSD-mimicking features
